preamble.summary: Proverbs 14 is the longest chapter in the Solomonic collection at thirty-five verses. It canvasses an extraordinary range of topics — the fear of the LORD, the reliability of witnesses, the nature of anger, the treatment of the poor, the deceptiveness of human self-assessment — weaving them into the persistent antithetic framework of wise versus foolish, righteous versus wicked. The chapter repeatedly returns to the question of what is real versus what merely appears to be real.
preamble.remarkable: This chapter contains two of the most quoted lines in Proverbs: 'There is a way that seems right to a person, but its end is the way of death' (verse 12) and 'In the fear of the LORD there is strong confidence' (verse 26). Verse 12 is so important that it is repeated verbatim in 16:25 — the only proverb in the Solomonic collection to appear twice in identical form. The chapter also contains a striking economic ethic: the one who oppresses the poor insults his Maker (verse 31), establishing a direct theological link between treatment of the vulnerable and relationship with God.
preamble.friction: Verse 4 — 'Where there are no oxen the manger is clean, but abundant harvest comes through the strength of an ox' — is a bracing piece of realism that cuts against any perfectionism. Productive life is messy. The clean manger produces nothing. Some readers may struggle with verse 13's observation that 'even in laughter the heart may be in pain' — the sages were not naive optimists but sharp observers of how people mask suffering with performance.
preamble.connections: The 'way that seems right' in verse 12 connects to the two-ways theology of Psalm 1 and Deuteronomy 30:15-20. The fear of the LORD as a 'fountain of life' (verse 27) echoes 13:14 and anticipates the programmatic statement of 1:7. The connection between oppressing the poor and insulting God (verse 31) parallels Job 31:13-15 and anticipates Matthew 25:40.

verses.text_hebrew: חַכְמ֣וֹת נָ֭שִׁים בָּנְתָ֣ה בֵיתָ֑הּ וְ֝אִוֶּ֗לֶת בְּיָדֶ֥יהָ תֶהֶרְסֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Every wise woman buildeth her house: but the foolish plucketh it down with her hands.
verses.rendering:
The wisest of women builds her household,
but a foolish woman tears it down with her own hands.
translator_notes: Chakhemot nashim ('wisdom of women, the wisest among women') is an intensive plural — the wisest of the wise among women. She bantah vetah ('has built her house') — the bayit ('house') is both the physical home and the family unit, the household as a social and economic institution. The ivvelet ('foolish woman') be-yadehah tehersennah ('with her own hands tears it down'). The destruction is self-inflicted and deliberate, though perhaps unintentional in its consequences.

verses.text_hebrew: הוֹלֵ֣ךְ בְּ֭יׇשְׁרוֹ יְרֵ֣א יְהוָ֑ה וּנְל֖וֹז דְּרָכָ֣יו בּוֹזֵֽהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: He that walketh in his uprightness feareth the LORD: but he that is perverse in his ways despiseth him.
verses.rendering:
The one who walks in uprightness fears the LORD,
but the one whose ways are crooked despises Him.
translator_notes: Walking be-yoshro ('in his uprightness') is equated with fearing the LORD — ethical conduct is the expression of reverence for God. The neloz derakhav ('one twisted in his paths, whose ways are crooked') is bozeihu ('one who despises Him') — moral crookedness is not merely poor behavior but active contempt for God.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּפִי־אֱ֭וִיל חֹ֣טֶר גַּאֲוָ֑ה וְשִׂפְתֵ֥י חֲ֝כָמִ֗ים תִּשְׁמוּרֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: In the mouth of the foolish is a rod of pride: but the lips of the wise shall preserve them.
verses.rendering:
In the mouth of a fool is a rod of arrogance,
but the lips of the wise protect them.
translator_notes: The choter ga'avah ('rod/shoot of pride') in the fool's mouth means his arrogant speech becomes a rod that beats him — his own words punish him. The wise person's lips tishmorem ('guard them, protect them') — careful speech is self-defense.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּאֵ֣ין אֲ֭לָפִים אֵב֣וּס בָּ֑ר וְרׇב־תְּ֝בוּא֗וֹת בְּכֹ֣חַ שֽׁוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: Where no oxen are, the crib is clean: but much increase is by the strength of the ox.
verses.rendering:
Where there are no oxen the feeding trough is clean,
but an abundant harvest comes through the strength of an ox.
verses.expanded_rendering: This proverb is a masterpiece of practical wisdom. The be-ein alafim ('where there are no oxen') the evus ('manger, feeding trough') is bar ('clean, empty') — no mess, no maintenance, no cost. But also no production. The rov tevu'ot ('abundance of produce, great harvest') comes be-khoach shor ('through the strength of an ox'). Productivity requires mess. The person who insists on a spotless operation will never produce anything of scale. The proverb validates the chaos that accompanies real work and warns against a sterile orderliness that avoids the costs of production.
translator_notes: This verse stands outside the typical righteous-versus-wicked framework and delivers pure agricultural pragmatism. Oxen eat, they defecate, they require care — but without them, the field cannot be plowed at scale. The clean manger is a sign of poverty, not efficiency.

verses.text_hebrew: עֵ֣ד אֱ֭מוּנִים לֹ֣א יְכַזֵּ֑ב וְיָפִ֥יחַ כְּ֝זָבִ֗ים עֵ֣ד שָֽׁקֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: A faithful witness will not lie: but a false witness will utter lies.
verses.rendering:
A faithful witness does not lie,
but a false witness breathes out deception.
translator_notes: The ed emunim ('faithful witness, reliable witness') lo yekhazzev ('does not deal falsely, does not lie') — his reliability is the negative: he simply does not do the thing that destroys trust. The ed shaqer ('witness of falsehood') yafiach kezavim ('breathes out lies') — deception flows from him as naturally as exhalation.

verses.text_hebrew: בִּקֶּשׁ־לֵ֣ץ חׇ֭כְמָה וָאָ֑יִן וְדַ֖עַת לְנָב֣וֹן נָקָֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: A scorner seeketh wisdom, and findeth it not: but knowledge is easy unto him that understandeth.
verses.rendering:
A scoffer searches for wisdom but finds nothing;
knowledge comes easily to the discerning.
translator_notes: The lets ('scoffer') searches for wisdom but va-ayin ('and there is nothing') — his contemptuous attitude blocks reception. Wisdom requires humility, and the scoffer has none. For the navon ('discerning, understanding person') knowledge is naqal ('easy, accessible, light') — not because it requires no effort but because the discerning person's posture makes learning natural.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֣ךְ מִ֭נֶּגֶד לְאִ֣ישׁ כְּסִ֑יל וּבַל־יָ֝דַ֗עְתָּ שִׂפְתֵי־דָֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: Go from the presence of a foolish man, when thou perceivest not in him the lips of knowledge.
verses.rendering:
Walk away from a foolish person,
for you will not find knowledgeable speech on his lips.
translator_notes: The imperative lekh mi-neged ('go from the front of, walk away from') is direct: leave the presence of a fool. The reason is pragmatic — bal yada'ta siftei da'at ('you will not know/find lips of knowledge') — there is nothing to learn there. Proximity to fools wastes time and may corrupt your own thinking.

verses.text_hebrew: חׇכְמַ֣ת עָ֭רוּם הָבִ֣ין דַּרְכּ֑וֹ וְאִוֶּ֖לֶת כְּסִילִ֣ים מִרְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The wisdom of the prudent is to understand his way: but the folly of fools is deceit.
verses.rendering:
The wisdom of the shrewd is to understand his own path;
the foolishness of fools is self-deception.
translator_notes: The arum ('shrewd, prudent') person's wisdom consists in havin darkko ('understanding his way') — knowing where he is going, why, and what the costs and consequences will be. Self-knowledge is the foundation. The kesilim ('fools') practice mirmah ('deceit') — but the context suggests they deceive themselves above all. Foolishness is fundamentally a failure of self-understanding.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱ֭וִלִים יָלִ֣יץ אָשָׁ֑ם וּבֵ֖ין יְשָׁרִ֣ים רָצֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: Fools make a mock at sin: but among the righteous there is favour.
verses.rendering:
Fools mock at guilt,
but among the upright there is goodwill.
translator_notes: The evilim ('fools') yalits asham ('mock at guilt, scoff at the guilt offering') — they treat moral failure as a joke, refusing to take the consequences of wrongdoing seriously. Among the yesharim ('upright'), by contrast, there is ratson ('favor, acceptance, goodwill') — right relationships produce mutual respect and generosity.

verses.text_hebrew: לֵ֗ב יוֹדֵ֥עַ מָרַּ֥ת נַפְשׁ֑וֹ וּ֝בְשִׂמְחָת֗וֹ לֹא־יִתְעָ֥רַב זָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: The heart knoweth his own bitterness; and a stranger doth not intermeddle with his joy.
verses.rendering:
The heart knows its own bitterness,
and no outsider can share in its joy.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is one of the most psychologically penetrating verses in the collection. The lev yode'a marrat nafsho ('the heart knows the bitterness of its own soul') — each person's suffering is ultimately private, inaccessible to others in its full depth. And be-simchato lo yit'arav zar ('in its joy a stranger does not mingle') — the same is true of joy. The deepest experiences of human life — both pain and delight — are finally solitary. No one can fully enter another person's inner world. The verse is not cynical but realistic: it acknowledges the fundamental privacy of consciousness and the limits of empathy.
translator_notes: This verse breaks from the antithetic pattern to make a single observation about the loneliness of inner experience. It anticipates modern psychology's recognition that subjective experience is ultimately incommunicable in its fullness.

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּ֣ית רְ֭שָׁעִים יִשָּׁמֵ֑ד וְאֹ֖הֶל יְשָׁרִ֣ים יַפְרִֽיחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: The house of the wicked shall be overthrown: but the tabernacle of the upright shall flourish.
verses.rendering:
The house of the wicked will be demolished,
but the tent of the upright will flourish.
translator_notes: The beit ('house') — suggesting permanence, solidity, stone construction — of the wicked will yishamed ('be destroyed, demolished'). The ohel ('tent') — suggesting fragility, impermanence, vulnerability — of the upright will yafriach ('flourish, blossom, bloom'). The reversal is deliberate: the seemingly solid is destroyed while the seemingly fragile thrives.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵ֤שׁ דֶּ֣רֶךְ יָ֭שָׁר לִפְנֵי־אִ֑ישׁ וְ֝אַחֲרִיתָ֗הּ דַּרְכֵי־מָֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: There is a way which seemeth right unto a man, but the end thereof are the ways of death.
verses.rendering:
There is a path that seems straight to a person,
but its end is the ways of death.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is perhaps the most sobering verse in the Solomonic collection — so important that it appears again verbatim in 16:25. The derekh yashar lifne ish ('a way straight before a person, a path that appears right') is not an obviously evil road. It seems right. It looks correct. The person walking it believes he is on solid ground. But acharitah darke mavet ('its end is the ways of death'). The plural 'ways of death' suggests multiple forms of destruction waiting at the terminus. The danger is not overt temptation but self-deception — the confident belief that one's own judgment is reliable when it may be catastrophically wrong. This verse is the antidote to moral self-assurance.
translator_notes: The word yashar ('straight, right, upright') is the same word used positively throughout Proverbs for moral uprightness. The irony is devastating: the path looks yashar but leads to death. Appearances of moral correctness can be fatally misleading.

The repetition of this exact verse in 16:25 is unique in the Solomonic collection. The sages considered this warning important enough to state twice — the danger of self-deception warranted double emphasis.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּם־בִּשְׂח֥וֹק יִכְאַב־לֵ֑ב וְ֝אַחֲרִיתָ֗הּ שִׂמְחָ֥ה תוּגָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Even in laughter the heart is sorrowful; and the end of that mirth is heaviness.
verses.rendering:
Even in laughter the heart may ache,
and joy may end in grief.
translator_notes: Gam bi-sechoq yikh'av lev ('even in laughter the heart is pained') — the sages observed that outward cheerfulness can mask inner suffering. People laugh while they hurt. And acharitah simchah tugah ('the end of joy is grief') — happiness does not last. This is not pessimism but observation: human emotional experience is layered and unstable.

verses.text_hebrew: מִדְּרָכָ֣יו יִ֭שְׂבַּע ס֣וּג לֵ֑ב וּ֝מֵעָלָ֗יו אִ֣ישׁ טֽוֹב׃
verses.text_kjv: The backslider in heart shall be filled with his own ways: and a good man shall be satisfied from himself.
verses.rendering:
The faithless in heart will have his fill of his own ways,
but a good person is satisfied from what is above him.
translator_notes: The sug lev ('turned-back of heart, faithless, backslider') will yisba mi-derakhav ('be filled with his own ways') — his own conduct will become his punishment, forced to consume the consequences of his choices. The ish tov ('good person') is satisfied me-alav ('from what is above him, from himself') — likely meaning from the fruit of his own good conduct, or from God who is above.

verses.text_hebrew: פֶּ֭תִי יַאֲמִ֣ין לְכׇל־דָּבָ֑ר וְ֝עָר֗וּם יָבִ֥ין לַאֲשֻׁרֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: The simple believeth every word: but the prudent man looketh well to his going.
verses.rendering:
The naive person believes everything,
but the shrewd person watches his step.
translator_notes: The peti ('naive, gullible, simple') ya'amin le-khol davar ('believes every word') — he has no filter, no critical faculty, no ability to distinguish reliable information from nonsense. The arum ('shrewd') yavin la-ashuro ('understands his step, pays attention to where he is going'). Critical thinking is a survival skill.

verses.text_hebrew: חָכָ֣ם יָ֭רֵא וְסָ֣ר מֵרָ֑ע וּ֝כְסִ֗יל מִתְעַבֵּ֥ר וּבוֹטֵֽחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: A wise man feareth, and departeth from evil: but the fool rageth, and is confident.
verses.rendering:
A wise person is cautious and turns from evil,
but a fool is reckless and overconfident.
translator_notes: The wise person yare ('fears, is cautious') and sar me-ra ('turns aside from evil') — his caution is not cowardice but prudence. The fool mit'abber ('crosses boundaries, acts recklessly') and bote'ach ('is confident, trusts himself'). False confidence is the fool's signature — he barges ahead where the wise person pauses.

verses.text_hebrew: קְֽצַר־אַ֭פַּיִם יַעֲשֶׂ֣ה אִוֶּ֑לֶת וְאִ֥ישׁ מְ֝זִמּ֗וֹת יִשָּׂנֵֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: He that is soon angry dealeth foolishly: and a man of wicked devices is hated.
verses.rendering:
A short-tempered person acts foolishly,
and a schemer is hated.
translator_notes: Qetsar appayim ('short of nostrils, quick to anger') — the Hebrew locates anger in the nose, as flaring nostrils. A person whose fuse is short ya'aseh ivvelet ('does foolishness') — anger produces bad decisions. The ish mezimmot ('person of schemes, plotter') yissane ('is hated') — his reputation for manipulation makes him socially toxic.

verses.text_hebrew: נָחֲל֣וּ פְתָאיִ֣ם אִוֶּ֑לֶת וַ֝עֲרוּמִ֗ים יַכְתִּ֥רוּ דָֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: The simple inherit folly: but the prudent are crowned with knowledge.
verses.rendering:
The naive inherit foolishness,
but the shrewd are crowned with knowledge.
translator_notes: The peta'im ('naive, gullible') nachalu ivvelet ('inherit foolishness') — folly is their estate, what they receive by default when they fail to pursue wisdom. The arumim ('shrewd') yaktiru da'at ('crown themselves with knowledge') — knowledge becomes their ateret ('crown, diadem'), a mark of honor and authority.

verses.text_hebrew: שַׁח֣וּ רָ֭עִים לִפְנֵ֣י טוֹבִ֑ים וּ֝רְשָׁעִ֗ים עַֽל־שַׁעֲרֵ֥י צַדִּֽיק׃
verses.text_kjv: The evil bow before the good; and the wicked at the gates of the righteous.
verses.rendering:
The evil will bow before the good,
and the wicked will stand at the gates of the righteous.
translator_notes: Shachu ra'im lifne tovim ('the evil bow before the good') — the moral order ultimately reasserts itself, reversing apparent power dynamics. The wicked end up as supplicants al sha'are tsaddiq ('at the gates of the righteous') — seeking what only the righteous can provide.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּם־לְ֭רֵעֵהוּ יִשָּׂנֵ֣א רָ֑שׁ וְאֹהֲבֵ֖י עָשִׁ֣יר רַבִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The poor is hated even of his own neighbour: but the rich hath many friends.
verses.rendering:
Even by his neighbor the poor person is disliked,
but the rich person has many friends.
translator_notes: This verse does not endorse the social dynamic it describes — it reports it. The rash ('poor person') yissane ('is disliked, hated') gam le-re'ehu ('even by his companion') — poverty isolates. The ashir ('rich person') has rabbim ohavim ('many who love him') — wealth attracts. The sages observed this pattern without approving it; subsequent verses (especially verse 31) will challenge it.

verses.text_hebrew: בָּז־לְרֵעֵ֥הוּ חוֹטֵ֑א וּמְחוֹנֵ֖ן (עֲנָוִ֣ים) [עֲנִיִּ֣ים] אַשְׁרָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: He that despiseth his neighbour sinneth: but he that hath mercy on the poor, happy is he.
verses.rendering:
Whoever despises his neighbor sins,
but whoever shows grace to the poor — how fortunate he is.
translator_notes: The baz le-re'ehu ('one who despises his neighbor') is choter ('sinning, missing the mark') — contempt for another person is a moral failure, not merely a social faux pas. The mechonen aniyyim ('one who shows grace to the poor') receives ashrav ('his blessedness, his happiness') — generosity toward the vulnerable produces human flourishing.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲ‍ֽלוֹא־יִ֭תְעוּ חֹ֣רְשֵׁי רָ֑ע וְחֶ֥סֶד וֶ֝אֱמֶ֗ת חֹ֣רְשֵׁי טֽוֹב׃
verses.text_kjv: Do they not err that devise evil? but mercy and truth shall be to them that devise good.
verses.rendering:
Do not those who plot evil go astray?
But faithful love and truth belong to those who plan good.
translator_notes: The rhetorical question expects a 'yes': the chorshe ra ('plotters of evil') yit'u ('go astray, wander, err'). Those who plan evil are themselves lost. The chorshe tov ('planners of good') receive chesed ve-emet ('faithful love and truth') — the paired divine attributes that characterize God's own covenant loyalty.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love and reliability, covenant devotion and truthfulness, steadfast mercy and faithfulness
key_terms.note: This word pair appears throughout the Hebrew Bible as a hendiadys describing God's covenant character (Exodus 34:6, Psalm 85:10). When it describes what comes to those who plan good, it suggests they receive the quality of relationship that mirrors God's own nature.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּכׇל־עֶ֭צֶב יִהְיֶ֣ה מוֹתָ֑ר וּדְבַר־שְׂ֝פָתַ֗יִם אַךְ־לְמַחְסֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: In all labour there is profit: but the talk of the lips tendeth only to penury.
verses.rendering:
In all hard work there is profit,
but mere talk leads only to poverty.
translator_notes: Be-khol etsev ('in all toil, in every painful exertion') yihyeh motar ('there will be surplus, profit, advantage'). Even difficult, unglamorous work produces results. But devar sefatayim ('word of lips, mere talk') akh le-machsor ('only to poverty, nothing but want'). Talk without action is economically catastrophic.

verses.text_hebrew: עֲטֶ֣רֶת חֲכָמִ֣ים עׇשְׁרָ֑ם אִוֶּ֖לֶת כְּסִילִ֣ים אִוֶּֽלֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: The crown of the wise is their riches: but the foolishness of fools is folly.
verses.rendering:
The crown of the wise is their wealth;
the garland of fools is foolishness.
translator_notes: The wise wear their oshram ('wealth') as an ateret ('crown') — their prosperity adorns them and reflects their competence. The fools' ivvelet ('foolishness') is their only crown — they have nothing to display but their own folly. The repetition of ivvelet in both halves of the line may be textually difficult; some emend the second to a different word, but the Masoretic text emphasizes: all fools have to show for themselves is more foolishness.

verses.text_hebrew: מַצִּ֣יל נְ֭פָשׁוֹת עֵ֣ד אֱמֶ֑ת וְיָפִ֖חַ כְּזָבִ֣ים מִרְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: A true witness delivereth souls: but a deceitful witness speaketh lies.
verses.rendering:
A truthful witness saves lives,
but a deceitful witness breathes out lies.
translator_notes: The ed emet ('witness of truth') is matsil nefashot ('rescuer of lives/souls') — honest testimony in court or community literally preserves people from wrongful punishment or unjust treatment. The yafiach kezavim ('breather-out of lies') is mirmah ('deceit, fraud') — his testimony endangers others.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּיִרְאַ֣ת יְ֭הוָה מִבְטַח־עֹ֑ז וּ֝לְבָנָ֗יו יִהְיֶ֥ה מַחְסֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: In the fear of the LORD is strong confidence: and his children shall have a place of refuge.
verses.rendering:
In the fear of the LORD there is strong confidence,
and his children will have a refuge.
verses.expanded_rendering: Be-yir'at Yahweh ('in the fear of the LORD') — the foundational disposition of wisdom — there is mivtach oz ('confidence of strength, strong security'). The paradox is deliberate: fear produces confidence. Reverent awe before God does not cripple a person but grounds him, providing the surest foundation for courage. And the benefit extends to the next generation: u-le-vanav yihyeh machseh ('and for his children there will be a refuge/shelter'). The parent who fears the LORD creates a safe space not only for himself but for his children. Faith becomes an inheritance of security.
translator_notes: The pairing of fear (yir'ah) and confidence (mivtach) is deliberately paradoxical. The person who fears God has nothing else to fear, because the ultimate authority is already his refuge. This is not servile terror but awe that produces stability.

verses.text_hebrew: יִרְאַ֣ת יְ֭הוָה מְק֣וֹר חַיִּ֑ים לָ֝ס֗וּר מִמֹּ֥קְשֵׁי מָֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: The fear of the LORD is a fountain of life, to depart from the snares of death.
verses.rendering:
The fear of the LORD is a fountain of life,
turning one away from the snares of death.
translator_notes: Nearly identical to 13:14, where 'the instruction of the wise' is the fountain of life. Here the fear of the LORD occupies the same structural position — suggesting that the fear of the LORD and wise instruction are functionally equivalent. Both are meqor chayyim ('fountain of life') and both serve lasur mi-moqshe mavet ('to turn aside from death's snares').

verses.text_hebrew: בְּרׇב־עָ֥ם הַדְרַת־מֶ֑לֶךְ וּבְאֶ֥פֶס לְ֝אֹ֗ם מְחִתַּ֥ת רָזֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: In the multitude of people is the king's honour: but in the want of people is the destruction of the prince.
verses.rendering:
A large population is a king's glory,
but without people a ruler is ruined.
translator_notes: Be-rov am ('in abundance of people') is hadrat melekh ('the glory/majesty of a king'). A ruler's power depends on his people — without subjects, a king is nothing. Be-efes le'om ('in the absence of a nation') is mechittat razon ('the ruin of a prince/ruler'). This is political realism: leadership exists in relationship to community.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶ֣רֶךְ אַ֭פַּיִם רַב־תְּבוּנָ֑ה וּקְצַר־ר֝֗וּחַ מֵרִ֥ים אִוֶּֽלֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: He that is slow to wrath is of great understanding: but he that is hasty of spirit exalteth folly.
verses.rendering:
The patient person has great understanding,
but the short-tempered person exalts foolishness.
translator_notes: Erekh appayim ('long of nostrils, slow to anger, patient') has rav tevunah ('great understanding, abundant discernment'). Patience is not passive but a sign of deep comprehension. The qetsar ruach ('short of spirit, hasty-tempered') merim ivvelet ('lifts up, exalts, elevates foolishness') — his impatience puts folly on a pedestal.

verses.text_hebrew: חַיֵּ֣י בְ֭שָׂרִים לֵ֣ב מַרְפֵּ֑א וּרְקַ֖ב עֲצָמ֣וֹת קִנְאָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: A sound heart is the life of the flesh: but envy the rottenness of the bones.
verses.rendering:
A tranquil heart is the life of the body,
but envy is rot in the bones.
translator_notes: Lev marpe ('a healing heart, a heart at peace, a tranquil heart') is chayyei vesarim ('the life of the flesh/body') — inner peace literally sustains physical health. Qin'ah ('envy, jealousy, passionate rivalry') is reqav atsamot ('rottenness of bones'). Envy does not merely distress the mind; it decays the body. The sages anticipated the psychosomatic insight that emotional states affect physical well-being.

verses.text_hebrew: עֹשֵׁ֣ק דָּ֭ל חֵרֵ֣ף עֹשֵׂ֑הוּ וּ֝מְכַבְּד֗וֹ חֹנֵ֥ן אֶבְיֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: He that oppresseth the poor reproacheth his Maker: but he that honoureth him hath mercy on the poor.
verses.rendering:
Whoever oppresses the poor insults his Maker,
but whoever shows grace to the needy honors Him.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse establishes one of the most radical theological connections in the wisdom literature. The osheq dal ('oppressor of the poor, one who exploits the vulnerable') is cheref osehu ('one who reproaches/insults his Maker') — to mistreat a poor person is to insult the God who made that person. The logic runs through the doctrine of creation: every human being is God's handiwork, and to damage what God has made is to show contempt for the craftsman. The reverse is equally direct: mekhabbedo ('the one who honors Him,' meaning God) is chonen evyon ('one who shows grace to the needy'). Generosity toward the vulnerable is not merely a social good but an act of worship.
translator_notes: The osehu ('his Maker') explicitly connects treatment of the poor to theology proper. God takes personally how people treat the vulnerable because the vulnerable bear His image. This principle resurfaces in Job 31:15 and in Jesus's parable of the sheep and goats (Matthew 25:40).

verses.text_hebrew: בְּ֭רָעָתוֹ יִדָּחֶ֣ה רָשָׁ֑ע וְחֹסֶ֖ה בְמוֹת֣וֹ צַדִּֽיק׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked is driven away in his wickedness: but the righteous hath hope in his death.
verses.rendering:
The wicked is overthrown by his own calamity,
but the righteous has refuge even in death.
translator_notes: The rasha is yiddacheh ('driven away, thrust out') be-ra'ato ('in his calamity/evil'). The righteous, however, choseh be-moto ('takes refuge in his death') — an extraordinary claim that trust in God extends even through death. Some scholars emend 'in his death' to 'in his integrity' (be-tummo), but the Masoretic text stands as one of the Hebrew Bible's rare hints at hope beyond death.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּלֵ֣ב נָ֭בוֹן תָּנ֣וּחַ חׇכְמָ֑ה וּבְקֶ֖רֶב כְּסִילִ֣ים תִּוָּדֵֽעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: Wisdom resteth in the heart of him that hath understanding: but that which is in the midst of fools is made known.
verses.rendering:
Wisdom rests quietly in the heart of the discerning,
but among fools it makes itself known.
translator_notes: Wisdom tanuach ('rests, settles, is at peace') in the heart of the navon ('discerning person') — it does not need to announce itself or prove itself. Among fools it tivvade'a ('makes itself known, is revealed') — possibly meaning that even among fools wisdom eventually becomes apparent, or that fools' lack of wisdom is obvious to all.

verses.text_hebrew: צְדָקָ֥ה תְרוֹמֵֽם־גּ֑וֹי וְחֶ֖סֶד לְאֻמִּ֣ים חַטָּֽאת׃
verses.text_kjv: Righteousness exalteth a nation: but sin is a reproach to any people.
verses.rendering:
Righteousness elevates a nation,
but sin is a disgrace to any people.
translator_notes: Tsedaqah teromem goy ('righteousness lifts up a nation') — this moves beyond individual ethics to political theology. National righteousness — justice, honesty, care for the vulnerable — elevates an entire people. Chattat ('sin, missing the mark') is chesed le'ummim — a much-debated phrase, likely meaning 'a disgrace/reproach to peoples.' National sin degrades a nation's standing and integrity.

verses.text_hebrew: רְצוֹן־מֶ֭לֶךְ לְעֶ֣בֶד מַשְׂכִּ֑יל וְ֝עֶבְרָת֗וֹ תִּהְיֶ֥ה מֵבִֽישׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: The king's favour is toward a wise servant: but his wrath is against him that causeth shame.
verses.rendering:
A king's favor rests on a servant who acts wisely,
but his fury falls on one who acts disgracefully.
translator_notes: The retson melekh ('king's favor') goes to the eved maskil ('servant who acts with insight, a competent servant'). The evrato ('his fury, his wrath') falls on the mevish ('one who causes shame, who acts disgracefully'). In the court setting, competence earns favor and incompetence earns wrath — a principle that applies beyond royal courts to any relationship where performance is evaluated.
